retrieved_docs.title: Edwards Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Craniofacial findings Skull: Microcephaly, bitemporal narrowing, and prominent occiput Face: Triangular and asymmetrical face with facial paralysis Eyes: Microphthalmia, hypertelorism, epicanthus, short palpebral fissures, coloboma of the iris, cataract, corneal clouding, hypoplastic supraorbital ridge, upward or downward slanting palpebral fissures, and abnormal retinal pigmentation. Nose: Prominent nasal bridge with hypoplastic nasal root, upturned nares, and choanal atresia Oral cavity: Micro-retrognathia, microstomia, narrow arched palate, cleft lip, and cleft palate Ears: Microtia, preauricular appendages, low-set or retroverted ears, and dysplastic ears [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Jacobsen Syndrome (11q Terminal Deletion Syndrome) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Most patients have developmental delays and facial dysmorphism, and all have some form of intellectual disability. A common cranial vault abnormality, metopic craniosynostosis, results in trigonocephaly, characterized by a triangularly shaped forehead and narrow temples. Facial features in Jacobsen syndrome can vary but commonly include hypertelorism, down-slanting palpebral fissures, strabismus, ptosis, sparse eyebrows, epicanthal folds, and eyelid coloboma. Other characteristic features include a flat or prominent nasal bridge, a short upturned nose, anteverted nares, prominent columella, a long or flat philtrum, a V-shaped mouth, and a thin upper lip. Skull and facial structures may also be affected, leading to micrognathia; retrognathia; or small, low-set, posteriorly rotated ears. [2] [19] Developmental disorders may manifest as attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, autism spectrum disorder, or severe behavioral disturbances. About 65% of patients have structural brain abnormalities, such as ventriculomegaly, cerebral atrophy, or agenesis of the corpus callosum. In addition, patients may experience hearing and vision deficits, necessitating a comprehensive ophthalmologic evaluation to assess for coloboma, cataracts, or retinal vessel abnormalities. [2]

retrieved_docs.content: Flat or prominent nasal bridge Small mandible Flat or prominent occiput Metopic ridge Low-set ears Large posterior fontanelle Malar hypoplasia Anteverted nose [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Cri Du Chat Syndrome -- History and Physical -- (a) Craniofacial Malformations
retrieved_docs.content: Microcephaly Moon face Hypertelorism Prominent epicanthic folds Large nasal bridge Downturned corners of the mouth Short philtrum Premature gray hair Abnormal transverse flexion creases Uncommonly: Downward slanting palpebral fissures Low-set ears Narrow auditory ducts Preauricular tags Deafness Myopia and cataracts Hypersensitivity of pupils to methacholine Hypospadias and cryptorchidism With increasing age, the following features change: Hypotonia in the neonatal period is replaced with hypertonia Prominent microcephaly Prominent supraorbital arch Dental malocclusions Moon face changes into a more narrow vertical face in adulthood

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Craniofacial Growth, And Development -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: For the neurocranium, the ability for displacement growth at suture lines is important for cranial growth. Early fusion of these suture lines is known as craniosynostosis. One condition where this occurs is Apert Syndrome. This syndrome is also characterized by midfacial hypoplasia with exorbitism, symphalangism, and syndactyly [11] . Another condition,  Alpert  syndrome, is primarily due to coronal suture premature fusion and leads to abnormal skull appearance and can also cause hydrocephalus and learning difficulties.

retrieved_docs.title: Faciocutaneoskeletal Syndrome (Costello Syndrome) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Craniofacial features Relative macrocephaly Low-set posteriorly rotated ears Relatively high forehead Full, thick eyebrows Dolichocilia Epicanthic folds Down-slanted palpebral fissures Wide nasal bridge Short nose Wide mouth Full lips Coarse facial features

retrieved_docs.title: Craniosynostosis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Apert syndrome: Apert syndrome is characterized by bi-coronal synostosis and turribrachycephaly. This syndrome presents with midface hypoplasia, hypertelorism, down-slanting palpebral fissures, cleft palate, and pseudoprognathic mandible. Other findings include syndactyly of hands and feet, and a short and broad thumb that is often radially deviated. Individuals with Apert syndrome may exhibit mild-to-moderate intellectual disabilities. [15] [26] [27] [28] Crouzon syndrome: Crouzon syndrome affects coronal, sagittal, or lambdoid sutures. Key features include midface hypoplasia, a beaked nose, exophthalmos, hypertelorism, and cervical vertebral fusion. Some patients can present with cleft lip or palate. These patients typically have normal intelligence. [15] [26] [27] [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Char Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Typical facial features include a depressed nasal bridge and broad flat nasal tip, widely spaced eyes, down-slanted palpebral fissures, mild ptosis, a short philtrum with prominent philtrum ridges with an upward pointing vermilion border resulting in a triangular mouth and thickened (patulous) everted lips. [3] The most commonly identified cardiac anomaly is PDA. [1] Less common findings include other types of congenital heart defects, other hand and foot anomalies, hypodontia, hearing loss, myopia and/or strabismus, polythelia, parasomnia, craniosynostosis (involving either the metopic or sagittal suture), and short stature. [1] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Oculo Auriculo Vertebral Spectrum -- History and Physical -- Oro-craniofacial Anomalies
retrieved_docs.content: These are commonly unilateral but can also have a bilateral asymmetrical presentation. The oro-facial anomalies include microsomia, macrostomia, cleft lip, cleft palate, malformation in the epiglottic fold, bifid uvula, jaw protrusion, and malocclusions. Dental abnormalities include delayed tooth development, agenesis of premolars and molars, enamel, and dentin malformations. Parents have also reported speech difficulties, feeding problems in infants, chewing and swallowing problems. Few authors have reported facial palsy, trigeminal anesthesia, and asymmetrical palatal elevation.

retrieved_docs.title: Apert Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with Apert syndrome typically present with craniosynostosis, midface hypoplasia, and symmetric syndactyly of the hands and feet (see Image . Cranioacial and Oral Findings in Apert Syndrome). The craniosynostosis is more severe compared to that seen in Crouzon syndrome, and the presence of syndactyly helps confirm the diagnosis. However, features such as hypertelorism (wide-set eyes), proptosis (bulging eyes), and down-slanting palpebral fissures are common in several craniosynostosis syndromes and cannot differentiate these conditions. Most patients also have a large anterior fontanelle displaced anteriorly. The midface is usually underdeveloped and retruded, leading to the underdevelopment of shallow orbits and down-slanting palpebral fissures. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Holoprosencephaly -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The severity of craniofacial defects often correlates with the degree of the intracranial anomaly.

retrieved_docs.title: Apert Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Other craniofacial deformities in Apert syndrome include acrocephaly (cone-shaped calvarium), proptosis, prominent forehead, hypertelorism, down-slanting palpebral fissures, and a flattened nasal bridge. Oral findings include dental crowding, high-arched palate, narrow palate, and pseudo-clefts. Skeletal abnormalities may include cervical vertebrae fusions, often involving C5-C6, scoliosis, atlantoaxial subluxation, or C1 spina bifida occulta. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Sotos syndrome, also known as cerebral gigantism, is another cause of megalencephaly. Other features include frontal bossing, a high forehead, high arched palate, intellectual disability or developmental delay, hypertelorism, and a prominent jaw or pointed chin. Most cases of Sotos syndrome result through sporadic mutations in the NSD1 gene.

retrieved_docs.title: Trigonocephaly -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Due to the suture's premature fusion, there is lateral growth restriction of the frontal bone with shortening of the anterior cranial fossa. [9] The fusion of the suture produces a forehead midline bone ridge. The orbits are located closer than normal due to restricted lateral growth. Bitemporal indentations are noted. The anterior fontanelle is closed in about half of the cases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized as a related group of adverse perinatal outcomes and are classified together as a distinct clinical category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2443
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively classified as an extremely premature birth of unspecified weight.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1843
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation are classified under the term تقييد النمو الجنيني, which denotes fetal growth restriction. This condition is understood to be part of a broader, unspecified disorder that originates in the perinatal period, referred to as حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة. Recognizing that تقييد النمو الجنيني falls within this larger perinatal context helps clinicians assess and manage infants presenting with these growth‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2314
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition; malpresentation is classified as a protracted (prolapsed) arm of the fetus, with the episode of care unspecified or not applicable.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 151
retrieved_docs.content: (unspecified condition originating in the perinatal period), situating fetal growth restriction within a larger, undefined perinatal condition category. Together, these relationships illustrate how gestational duration, low birth weight, fetal growth restriction, and associated perinatal disorders are organized within medical classification systems, aiding clinicians in diagnosing and managing newborns with short gestation and related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: فالج is a general medical condition, and فالج طفلي represents a more specific, narrower form of فالج, indicating the infantile variant of this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 944
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal growth restriction (تقييد النمو الجنيني) is described as having a broader relationship with an unspecified condition that originated in the perinatal period (حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة), indicating that fetal growth restriction can be viewed as a more general clinical category under which certain undefined perinatal conditions may be classified, underscoring the importance of thorough assessment of growth abnormalities around the time of birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2763
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation constitute a severe perinatal condition that is classified as an extremely premature birth of the highest degree with unspecified weight (ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}). This classification highlights that the infant is born far before term, exhibits markedly reduced body weight, and shows evidence of impaired intra‑uterine growth, all of which place the newborn at high risk for morbidity and mortality and necessitate intensive neonatal monitoring and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2901
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation is recognized as a specific perinatal condition that is classified as تقييد النمو الجنيني, indicating fetal growth restriction, and also as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}, denoting a birth at the highest degree of prematurity with an unspecified weight. This dual classification underscores the clinical importance of identifying both the limited fetal growth and the extreme prematurity associated with these presentations for appropriate medical management and follow‑up.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2873
retrieved_docs.content: The condition referred to as اضطراب جنيني (fetal disorder) is classified as a type of مضاعفات الحمل (pregnancy complication). Recognizing that fetal disorders are encompassed within the broader category of pregnancy complications helps clinicians identify, monitor, and manage these issues during prenatal care, ensuring appropriate assessment and intervention for both maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively classified as تقييد النمو الجنيني, a condition denoting fetal growth restriction. This classification highlights the interrelated nature of these perinatal indicators, which together signal impaired intrauterine growth and may necessitate focused obstetric monitoring and intervention to improve neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2818
retrieved_docs.content: Conditions of fetal growth and malnutrition are regarded as a parent category that includes the specific outcomes of short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. The grouping of short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation serves as a classification label for these adverse perinatal conditions, emphasizing their interrelated nature in the context of impaired fetal development and inadequate nutrition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), indicating that children born prematurely with low birth weight have an increased risk of developing reduced height later in life.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2270
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical presentation described as “Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation” is used to define and classify that same set of perinatal abnormalities, and it is categorized under the term “تقييد النمو الجنيني,” which translates to fetal growth restriction. This grouping emphasizes that short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively recognized as a single entity of fetal growth restriction in medical classification.
